issues.original: Greedy is the algorithm paradigm that seems too good to be true: at every step, choose the option that looks best right now, never look back, and somehow reach the global optimum. When it works, the code is almost embarrassingly short. When it doesn't, it produces confidently wrong answers — which is why the real skill is recognizing when greedy is allowed.
issues.fixed: The greedy algorithm paradigm seems too good to be true: at every step, choose the option that looks best right now, never look back, and somehow reach the global optimum. When it works, the code is almost embarrassingly short. When it doesn't, it produces confidently wrong answers — which is why the real skill is recognizing when greedy is allowed.

issues.original: A **greedy algorithm** builds a solution incrementally by committing, at each step, to the choice that is locally best according to some criterion — and **never undoing it**. That last clause is what separates greedy from backtracking and from DP: there is no [CODE], no memo table of alternative branches.
issues.fixed: A **greedy algorithm** builds a solution incrementally by committing, at each step, to the choice that is locally best according to some criterion — and **never undoing it**. That last clause is what separates greedy from backtracking and DP: there is no [CODE], no memo table of alternative branches.

issues.original: For this strategy to be correct, the problem must have two structural properties: - **Greedy choice property.** There exists a locally optimal choice that is part of *some* globally optimal solution. (Equivalently: making this choice never destroys the best possible future.) - **Optimal substructure.** Once that choice is committed, the remainder of the problem is itself a smaller instance of the same problem, and an optimal solution for it combines with the greedy choice into an optimal solution for the original.
issues.fixed: For this strategy to be correct, the problem must have two structural properties: - **Greedy choice property.** There exists a locally optimal choice that is part of *some* globally optimal solution. (Equivalently: making this choice never destroys the best possible future.) - **Optimal substructure.** Once the choice is committed, the remainder of the problem is a smaller instance of the same problem, and an optimal solution for it combines with the greedy choice to form an optimal solution for the original.

issues.original: Most problems satisfy optimal substructure. The greedy choice property is the rarer one, and it is the part that needs a proof. **If you cannot prove the greedy choice property, you do not have a greedy algorithm — you have a guess.**
issues.fixed: Most problems satisfy optimal substructure. The greedy choice property is rarer and requires a proof. **If you cannot prove the greedy choice property, you do not have a greedy algorithm — you have a guess.**

issues.original: The takeaway: **greedy trades correctness for runtime**. You pay in proof effort and are rewarded with linear or near-linear time.
issues.fixed: The takeaway: **greedy trades correctness for runtime**. You pay in proof effort and get linear or near-linear time in return.

issues.original: Coin change is the canonical cautionary tale. With the canonical US coin set [CODE], "always take the largest coin that fits" produces an optimal answer for every amount. With a slightly different set [CODE] and amount [CODE], greedy picks [CODE] (three coins) while the optimal is [CODE] (two coins). Same algorithm, same intuition — but the second instance lacks the structural property (technically: the coin set is not a *matroid*) that makes greedy correct.
issues.fixed: Coin change is the canonical cautionary tale. With the canonical US coin set [CODE], "always take the largest coin that fits" produces an optimal answer for every amount. With a slightly different set [CODE] and amount [CODE], greedy picks [CODE] (three coins) while the optimal is [CODE] (two coins). The same algorithm and intuition apply, but the second instance lacks the structural property (technically: the coin set is not a *matroid*) that makes greedy correct.

issues.original: The lesson: **greedy correctness is a property of the problem, not the algorithm**. Always either prove it or recognize it from a known pattern.
issues.fixed: The lesson: **greedy correctness is a property of the problem, not the algorithm**. Always prove it or recognize it from a known pattern.

issues.original: Across LeetCode, greedy solutions tend to fall into a small number of recurring shapes: - **Sort by end** — interval scheduling, non-overlapping intervals, minimum-arrows. - **Sort by start** — meeting-room allocation, merging intervals. - **Sort by ratio / value density** — fractional knapsack, profit-weighted scheduling. - **Maintain a running extremum** — Jump Game ([CODE]), Best Time to Buy/Sell Stock ([CODE] so far), Maximum Subarray ([CODE]). - **Prefix-sum reset** — Gas Station, Maximum Subarray (Kadane), Partition Labels (last-occurrence sweep). - **Frequency / heap-based scheduling** — Task Scheduler, Reorganize String. - **Local adjustment in two passes** — Candy, Wiggle Subsequence.
issues.fixed: Across LeetCode, greedy solutions tend to fall into a small number of recurring shapes: - **Sort by end** — interval scheduling, non-overlapping intervals, minimum-arrows. - **Sort by start** — meeting-room allocation, merging intervals. - **Sort by ratio / value density** — fractional knapsack, profit-weighted scheduling. - **Maintain a running extremum** — Jump Game ([CODE]), Best Time to Buy/Sell Stock ([CODE] so far), Maximum Subarray ([CODE]). - **Prefix-sum reset** — Gas Station, Maximum Subarray (Kadane), Partition Labels (last-occurrence sweep). - **Frequency / heap-based scheduling** — Task Scheduler, Reorganize String. - **Local adjustment in two passes** — Candy, Wiggle Subsequence.

issues.original: The most common proof technique for greedy is the **exchange argument**: 1. Take any optimal solution [CODE]. 2. Show that you can replace the first non-greedy choice in [CODE] with the greedy choice, producing a new solution [CODE] that is **no worse**. 3. By induction, [CODE] can be transformed step by step into the greedy solution without losing optimality. 4. Therefore the greedy solution is itself optimal.
issues.fixed: The most common proof technique for greedy is the **exchange argument**: 1. Take any optimal solution [CODE]. 2. Show that you can replace the first non-greedy choice in [CODE] with the greedy choice, producing a new solution [CODE] that is **no worse**. 3. By induction, [CODE] can be transformed step by step into the greedy solution without losing optimality. 4. Therefore, the greedy solution is optimal.

issues.original: Worked example for interval scheduling: [CODE] schedules some interval [CODE] first, but the greedy choice [CODE] is the interval with the earliest finish time. Because [CODE], every interval that was compatible with [CODE] (and came after it) is still compatible with [CODE]. Swap [CODE] for [CODE]; size is unchanged, feasibility is preserved. Repeat for the second pick, the third, and so on. The greedy schedule has the same size as [CODE], so it is optimal.

Because [CODE], every interval that was compatible with [CODE] (and came after it) is still compatible with [CODE]. Swap [CODE] for [CODE]; the size remains unchanged, and feasibility is preserved. Repeat for the second pick, the third, and so on. The greedy schedule has the same size as [CODE], so it is optimal.

issues.original: Backtracking is the algorithm you use when a problem asks you to *enumerate* something — every permutation, every subset, every legal board, every path through a grid. It's brute force with a brain: you build a candidate solution one decision at a time, abandon it as soon as a constraint says 'this won't work,' and undo your last move so the next branch starts fresh.
issues.fixed: Backtracking is the algorithm you use when a problem asks you to *enumerate* something — every permutation, every subset, every legal board, every path through a grid. It's brute force with a brain: you build a candidate solution one decision at a time, abandon it as soon as a constraint says 'this won't work,' and undo your last move to start the next branch fresh.

issues.original: This simple pattern solves Permutations, Combinations, Subsets, Word Search, N-Queens, and Sudoku, and it's the same template you'll use for about 90% of 'find all...' problems on LeetCode.
issues.fixed: This simple pattern solves Permutations, Combinations, Subsets, Word Search, N-Queens, and Sudoku, and it's the same template you'll use for about 90% of 'find all...' problems on LeetCode.

issues.original: Backtracking is depth-first search over an *implicit* tree of partial solutions. Each node is a partial answer (the [CODE] so far). Each edge is a single decision (add element [CODE], place a queen on column [CODE], write digit [CODE] in this cell). When you reach a leaf that satisfies the goal, you record it. When you reach a node that violates a constraint, you stop descending and back up. The 'back' in backtracking is the explicit *undoing* of the last decision so that the algorithm can try a sibling branch with the state it had before.
issues.fixed: Backtracking is depth-first search over an *implicit* tree of partial solutions. Each node is a partial answer (the [CODE] so far). Each edge is a single decision (add element [CODE], place a queen on column [CODE], write digit [CODE] in this cell). When you reach a leaf that satisfies the goal, you record it. When you reach a node that violates a constraint, you stop descending and back up. The 'back' in backtracking is the explicit *undoing* of the last decision so the algorithm can try a sibling branch with the previous state.

issues.original:
When to reach for backtracking:
- The problem says 'return **all** ...' (permutations, combinations, subsets, partitions, parenthesizations).
- It is a constraint-satisfaction problem (N-Queens, Sudoku, graph coloring).
- The search space is exponential but most of it can be eliminated by checking partial constraints early.
- You need *one* solution and a witness, and a polynomial-time greedy / DP doesn't apply.
issues.fixed:
When to reach for backtracking:
- The problem says 'return **all** ...' (permutations, combinations, subsets, partitions, parenthesizations).
- It is a constraint-satisfaction problem (N-Queens, Sudoku, graph coloring).
- The search space is exponential, but most of it can be eliminated by checking partial constraints early.
- You need *one* solution and a witness, and a polynomial-time greedy or DP doesn't apply.

issues.original:
When **not** to reach for it:
- You only need a count or an optimal value, and the subproblems overlap -> use DP.
- The search space is small enough that you can enumerate states iteratively.
- The problem reduces to BFS shortest path or a known graph algorithm.
issues.fixed:
When **not** to reach for it:
- You only need a count or an optimal value, and the subproblems overlap -> use DP.
- The search space is small enough to enumerate states iteratively.
- The problem reduces to BFS shortest path or a known graph algorithm.

issues.original: DFS *traverses*: it visits each node once, never modifies what it walked over, and the recursion stack alone holds enough state. Backtracking *constructs*: the same [CODE] is mutated all the way down and restored all the way up, so the algorithm can reuse it across exponentially many branches without copying. Backtracking uses DFS as its motion, but adds the choose/un-choose discipline.
issues.fixed: DFS *traverses*: it visits each node once, never modifies what it walked over, and the recursion stack alone holds enough state. Backtracking *constructs*: the same [CODE] is mutated all the way down and restored all the way up, so the algorithm can reuse it across exponentially many branches without copying. Backtracking uses DFS for motion but adds the choose/un-choose discipline.

issues.original: A binary tree problem is rarely about the tree itself. It's about *the order in which you visit nodes* and *what you remember from the children before deciding what to do at the parent*. Once these two ideas click, the four traversal orders, iterative rewrites, construction problems, and even classics like Validate BST and Maximum Depth all boil down to variations on the same recipe. This article builds that recipe from start to finish.
issues.fixed: A binary tree problem is rarely about the tree itself. It's about *the order in which you visit nodes* and *what you remember from the children before deciding what to do at the parent*. Once these two ideas click, the four traversal orders, iterative rewrites, construction problems, and even classics like Validate BST and Maximum Depth all boil down to variations of the same recipe. This article builds that recipe from start to finish.

issues.original: We use a single example tree throughout, so the figures and code align perfectly.

issues.original: A **binary tree** is a tree where each node has at most two children, and the children are *ordered* — left and right are not interchangeable. An empty tree is also considered a binary tree, serving as the base case for recursive definitions.
issues.fixed: A **binary tree** is a tree where each node has at most two children, and the children are *ordered* — left and right are not interchangeable. An empty tree is also considered a binary tree, serving as the base case for recursive definitions.

issues.original: Three labels appear over and over in problem statements: - **Root** — the unique node with no parent. - **Internal node** — any node with at least one child. - **Leaf** — a node with no children. And two numbers describe how "tall" a node sits inside the tree: - The **depth** of a node is the number of edges from the root to it. The root has depth [CODE]. - The **height** of a node is the number of edges on the longest downward path to any leaf below it. A leaf has height [CODE]. The height of the *tree* is the height of the root.

Two numbers describe how "tall" a node sits inside the tree: - The **depth** of a node is the number of edges from the root to it. The root has depth [CODE]. - The **height** of a node is the number of edges on the longest downward path to any leaf below it. A leaf has height [CODE]. The height of the *tree* is the height of the root.

issues.original: The minimal Python representation is a class with a value and two child pointers: That is the entire data model. Everything below is some way of walking it.
issues.fixed: The minimal Python representation is a class with a value and two child pointers. That is the entire data model. Everything below is some way of walking it.

issues.original: A few special shapes show up as constraints in problem statements; recognising them often unlocks an asymptotically better solution. - **Full binary tree** — every node has either [CODE] or [CODE] children, never exactly one. Common in expression trees. - **Complete binary tree** — every level is full except possibly the last, which fills from left to right. Heaps are stored this way, which lets you implement them in a flat array. - **Balanced binary tree** — for every node, the heights of the two subtrees differ by at most one. This is the property AVL and red-black trees maintain to keep operations [FORMULA]. - **Binary search tree (BST)** — for every node, *every* value in the left subtree is strictly less than the node and *every* value in the right subtree is strictly greater. We will exploit this directly when validating a BST.
issues.fixed: A few special shapes show up as constraints in problem statements; recognizing them often unlocks an asymptotically better solution. - **Full binary tree** — every node has either [CODE] or [CODE] children, never exactly one. Common in expression trees. - **Complete binary tree** — every level is full except possibly the last, which fills from left to right. Heaps are stored this way, allowing implementation in a flat array. - **Balanced binary tree** — for every node, the heights of the two subtrees differ by at most one. This is the property AVL and red-black trees maintain to keep operations [FORMULA]. - **Binary search tree (BST)** — for every node, *every* value in the left subtree is strictly less than the node, and *every* value in the right subtree is strictly greater. We will exploit this directly when validating a BST.

issues.original: Every depth-first traversal performs three actions at each node — visit the node, recurse left, recurse right — but in different orders. The order is what gives the traversal its name: | Order | Action sequence | Resulting visit list on our tree | --- | --- | --- | | **Preorder** | root → left → right | [CODE] | | **Inorder** | left → root → right | [CODE] | | **Postorder** | left → right → root | [CODE] | Figure 2 shows all three on the same tree, with each node numbered by its visit step.
issues.fixed: Every depth-first traversal performs three actions at each node — visit the node, recurse left, and recurse right — but in different orders. The order is what gives the traversal its name: | Order | Action sequence | Resulting visit list on our tree | --- | --- | --- | | **Preorder** | root → left → right | [CODE] | | **Inorder** | left → root → right | [CODE] | | **Postorder** | left → right → root | [CODE] | Figure 2 shows all three on the same tree, with each node numbered by its visit step.

issues.original: The "where does the root sit" rule is the entire trick: the root is processed *first* in preorder, *between* the two children in inorder, and *last* in postorder. The recursive code makes this almost too obvious: The choice of order is dictated by *when you have the information you need*. Preorder is right when a parent decision must happen before its children — copying a tree, printing a directory tree, encoding for serialization. Inorder is right whenever the BST ordering matters — sorted output, validation, the [FORMULA]-th smallest. Postorder is right when a parent's answer is built from its children's answers — deleting a tree, computing subtree sums or sizes, and (as we'll see) computing height.
issues.fixed: The "where does the root sit" rule is the key: the root is processed *first* in preorder, *between* the two children in inorder, and *last* in postorder. The recursive code makes this almost too obvious. The choice of order depends on *when you have the information you need*. Preorder is used when a parent decision must happen before its children, such as copying a tree, printing a directory tree, or encoding for serialization. Inorder is used when the BST ordering matters, such as for sorted output, validation, or finding the [FORMULA]-th smallest. Postorder is used when a parent's answer is built from its children's answers, such as deleting a tree, computing subtree sums or sizes, and (as we'll see) computing height.

issues.original: A linked list is the simplest data structure that forces you to **think in pointers**. Arrays let you index in [FORMULA] and forget about layout; linked lists hand you a head pointer and ask, *"now what?"* That single shift — from indices to references — is what makes linked-list problems so common in interviews. They are short to state, brutal to get right, and reward exactly the habits good engineers build: drawing pictures, naming pointers, and **never dereferencing without checking for [CODE]**.
issues.fixed: A linked list is the simplest data structure that forces you to **think in pointers**. Arrays let you index with [FORMULA] and forget about layout; linked lists give you a head pointer and ask, *"now what?"* That shift — from indices to references — is why linked-list problems are so common in interviews. They are easy to state, hard to get right, and reward the habits good engineers build: drawing pictures, naming pointers, and **never dereferencing without checking for [CODE]**.

issues.original: The trade-off is concrete: arrays win on access and cache behavior, linked lists win when you mutate the structure often and do not need to jump around. Once you internalize this, problem selection becomes obvious — *"can I reach the kth element?"* points to an array; *"do I need to splice out the middle without copying?"* points to a list.

issues.original: If you have ever caught yourself writing a double [CODE] loop to inspect every contiguous subarray, **sliding window** is probably the optimisation you are missing. It turns an [FORMULA] or [FORMULA] scan into a single linear pass by *reusing the work* it has already done. This article walks through the technique from first principles, then drills four canonical LeetCode problems plus a monotonic-deque variant.
issues.fixed: If you've ever written a double [CODE] loop to inspect every contiguous subarray, **sliding window** is likely the optimization you're missing. It transforms an [FORMULA] or [FORMULA] scan into a single linear pass by reusing the work it has already done. This article explains the technique from first principles and then covers four canonical LeetCode problems plus a monotonic-deque variant.

issues.original: A sliding window is a contiguous range [CODE] over an array or string. Instead of recomputing everything when the range moves, we **add the element entering on the right** and **remove the element leaving on the left**. Each element is touched at most twice, so the total cost is [FORMULA].

Instead of recomputing everything when the range moves, we **add the element entering on the right** and **remove the element leaving on the left**. Each element is touched at most twice, making the total cost [FORMULA].

issues.original: There are two flavours: - **Fixed-size window** — width is a constant [CODE] and both pointers advance together. - **Variable-size window** — [CODE] expands to grow the window, [CODE] contracts to repair it when an invariant breaks.
issues.fixed: There are two types: - **Fixed-size window** — the width is a constant [CODE], and both pointers advance together. - **Variable-size window** — [CODE] expands to grow the window, and [CODE] contracts to repair it when an invariant breaks.

issues.original: The orange cell is *entering* the window; the pink one is *leaving*. Notice the right column: each step is one subtraction and one addition, never a fresh [CODE] call. That is the entire trick.
issues.fixed: The orange cell is *entering* the window, and the pink one is *leaving*. Notice the right column: each step involves one subtraction and one addition, never a fresh [CODE] call. That's the entire trick.

issues.original: When the window size is not given, we use two pointers and let the problem dictate the policy: - **Find the longest valid window:** keep expanding [CODE]; whenever the window goes invalid, contract [CODE] until it is valid again, then record the length. - **Find the shortest valid window:** keep expanding [CODE] until the window becomes valid; then contract [CODE] as far as possible while staying valid, recording the length on each shrink.

issues.original: The crucial invariant: **[CODE] only ever moves right**. So even though there is a [CODE] inside a [CODE], the inner loop's *total* work across the whole run is bounded by [CODE]. That is the amortised-[FORMULA] argument.

issues.original: Stacks and queues look unassuming next to graphs or DP, but they sit underneath an astonishing fraction of interview problems. The reason is simple: most algorithmic questions are really questions about *order of access*. Stacks give you LIFO (last in, first out); queues give you FIFO (first in, first out); and once you add the variants — monotonic stack, deque, priority queue — you have efficient answers for bracket matching, next-greater-element, sliding-window extrema, top-K, BFS, and a long tail of "implement X using Y" puzzles.
issues.fixed: Stacks and queues may seem unassuming next to graphs or dynamic programming, but they underpin a surprising number of interview problems. The reason is simple: most algorithmic questions are really about *order of access*. Stacks provide LIFO (last in, first out), while queues offer FIFO (first in, first out). Add variants like monotonic stacks, deques, and priority queues, and you can efficiently solve problems like bracket matching, next-greater-element, sliding-window extrema, top-K, BFS, and a long list of "implement X using Y" puzzles.

issues.original: The key observation is the *nesting* rule: when a closing bracket arrives, it must match the **most recently opened** one that is still unclosed. "Most recently opened" is exactly what a stack tracks.

issues.original: Hash tables buy you speed by spending memory. Two pointers is the opposite trade: spend a little structural assumption — the array is sorted, the list might have a cycle, the answer lives in a contiguous window — and you get [FORMULA] time with [FORMULA] extra space.

issues.original: Two pointers [CODE] and [CODE] start at the ends of a sorted array and walk toward each other. The invariant is: > The answer, if it exists, lies in the closed interval [CODE]. A comparison either confirms the current pair or rules out one of the boundary elements. The pointer at the ruled-out element moves; the other stays.

issues.original: Sorting groups duplicates; the algorithm has to walk *past* each duplicate group at every level it picks from: - **First slot.** [CODE] — never pick the same outer value twice. - **Second slot.** After a hit, advance [CODE] while the new value equals the just-used value. - **Third slot.** Same idea on the right.
